content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
October 1, 1999
Eleanore Hahn
City of Belmont
Belmont City Council
1070 Sixth Avenue
Belmont, California 94002-3893
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-239
Dear Ms. Hahn:
This letter responds to your request for advice about the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”).
1
I. QUESTIONS
1. Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions regarding a lawsuit by the
City of Belmont against a private homeowners’ association, “The Knolls of Belmont”?
2. Does the Act prohibit you from taking real estate listings for properties in a certain
development, “Bayview at Belmont,” merely because you voted in the July 1999 city council
decision involving two particular properties in that development?
II. CONCLUSIONS
1. The answer to this question depends primarily on whether council decisions about The
Knolls lawsuit will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the individual who
purchased your former residence in The Knolls, and who is thus a source of income to you.
Although we cannot make this essentially factual determination from a distance, we have
explained the specific factual questions you must answer to make this determination.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-239
Page 2
2. No. Merely having made a governmental decision about Bayview in the past does not,
in and of itself, preclude you from doing business in Bayview in the future.
III. FACTS
You are a city councihnember for the City of Belmont (the “city”). In your private life,
you work in real estate sales. You are associated with a real estate brokerage, Rupert Ta’lor, as
an independent contractor.
A. Facts related to question no. 1.
In early 1998, there were several severe slides in the hills of Belmont. One of the slides
occurred in the Hallmark area of Belmont when a portion of the hillside belonging to a private
homeowners’ association, “The Knolls ofBelmont” (The Knolls), fell into and clogged a
drainage ditch (“V” Ditch). The clogged drainage ditch caused water to cascade onto the hill
below, weakening the hill and causing a portion of the hillside to cave into a home, thus
destroying the rear master bedroom and bath area ofthat home.
The city awarded a contract for repair of this bill and below the “V” Ditch to prevent a
future occurrence. The lower wall has been built; however, the upper wall cannot be constructed
without permission from The Knolls, since the upper wall would be on their property. The
Board of The Knolls instructed their attorney to not communicate in any way with Belmont’s city
attorney. A lawsuit was filed by the City ofBelmont against The Knolls, claiming negligence.
You anticipate that you will take real estate listings in The Knolls in the near future;
however, at the present time, you have no listings there. Rupert Taylor has no particular
connection with the lawsuit, or The Knolls.
Within the past twelve months, you sold (as seller, not agent) your former personal
residence in The Knolls. The buyer of this home still lives in the home, and is a member of The
Knolls homeowners’ association.
B. Facts related to question no. 2.
In July 1999, you voted, as a city council member, on a matter involving the construction
of sidewallcs in front of two houses in a development called “Bayview at Belmont” (Bayview).


File No. A-99-239
Page 3
IV. ANALYSIS
A.
Introduction.
The Act’s conflict-ofinterest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own fmancial interests or the
financial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or othrwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
To say that a public official has a “financial interest” in a, governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, is to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official’s economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard,
eight-step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualif’ing conflict of interest in a
given governmental decision.
2 (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) The following advice applies that
standard analysis.
B.
The lawsuit involving The Knolls.
1.
You are covered by the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to “public officials.” (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) As a city coundil member, you are a “public official,”
for purposes of the Act (see Sections 82048, 82041), and the conflict-of-interest rules apply to
you.
2. Decisions about the lawsuit against The Knolls are covered by the Act’s conflict-of-
interest provisions.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official “make[sj,
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.”
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) The Commission has adopted a series of regulations
which defme “making,” “participating in making,” and “influencing” a governmental decision,
and which provide certain exceptions. (Regulations 18702-18702.4.)
2 The eighth step, which pertains to the “legally required participation” rule (see Regulation 18708),
applies only in rare cases where several public officials in the same agency are simultaneously disqualified. It is not
relevant to this advice request, and is not mentioned further.


File No. A-99-239
Page 4
By deliberating and voting in council decisions about the lawsuit against The Knolls, you
would be making (Regulation 18702.1), and participating in making (Regulation 18702.2), a
governmental decision. Thus, the Act’s conflict-of-interest rules apply to these decisions.
3. Identifying your relevant economic interests.
(a) Introduction
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The “economic interests” from which conflicts of interest may arise are defined in
Regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifying which, if any, ofthese economic interests are held by a
public official is the third step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest under the Act. (See
Regulation 18700(b)(3).) There are five kinds of such economic interests:
•
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment
3 of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position ofmanagement (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));
•
A public official has an economic interest in realproperty in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofincome, including promised
income, which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofgfts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4);
•
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those ofhis or her immediate family—this is known as the
“personal financial effects” rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).
An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the
official, the official’s agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)


File No. A-99-239
Page 5
(b) You have an economic interest in the person who bought your house in The Knolls.
When you sold your residence in The Knolls, the buyer became a source of income to
you. (See Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) Thus, you have an economic interest in that
buyer for twelve months following the transaction.
4
There are no facts, however, which indicate that you have an economic interest in The
Knolls itself (i.e., as a legal entity). Absent facts that indicate, for example, that the buyer of
your residence controls the homeowners’ association or faces unusual or unique liability in the
lawsuit, or that The Knolls has been a client of yours, there is no basis to conclude that you have
an economic interest in The Knolls as a legal entity.
(c) You have an economic interest in Rupert Taylor, the brokerage.
As an independent contractor, you have received income from the broker with whom you
are assoóiated, Rupert Taylor. Presumably, that income is at least $250 in the past twelve
months. Therefore, you have an economic interest in Rupert Taylor because it is a source of
income to you. (See Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
(d) Thefact that you may have listings in The Knolls in thefuture does not create any
economic interestsfor you right now.
You have asked about whether the fact that you may have real estate listings in The
Knolls in the future is relevant to whether you have a conflict of interst in the present decision
about the lawsuit against The Knolls. A public officiallreal estate agent’s listings can be relevant
because listings lead to income from the buying and selling of the listed property. “Income”
under the Act includes promised income, that is, income to which a public official has a legally
enforceable right. The person against whom the public official may enforce this legal right is the
source of the income.
We have consistently advised that merely having a listing on a property does not
constitute promised income. However, once a sale is pending on the listed property, the client is
deemed to be a source of promised income.
5 Merely having a listing on a property does not
constitute promised income because a listing is too speculative to rise to the level ofpromised
income. However, once escrow is opened, there is a buyer and a seller, and the element of
speculation has diminished.
Although a public official need not disclose his or her personal residence on his or her Statement of
Economic Interests (FPPC Form 700) (See Section 87206(f)), a personal residence may nonetheless cause a conflict
of interest. In other words, although you may disregard your personal residence for disclosure purposes, you must
consider it for purposes of deciding whether you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given decision.
See the Bergman Advice Letter, No. A-93-297, Clay Advice Letter, No. 1-96-33 5, Leichter Advice
Letter, No. 1-98-173, and the Weiler Advice Letter, No. A-99-060.


File No. A-99-239
Page 6
4. Determining the degree to which your economic interests are involved in the lawsuit
against The Knolls.
The fourth step in analyzing whether you have a conflict of interest is to determine
whether your respective economic interests are directly or indirectly involved in the decisions
about The Knolls lawsuit. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) This step is important because it helps
determine (in the flflh step) which test for materiality to use in deciding whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the economic interets.
In this case, your two relevant economic interests, the buyer of your former personal
residence and Rupert Taylor are sources of income to you. A source of income is directly
involved in a decision when that person:
•
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704.1(a)(1)); or
•
Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation 18704.1 (a)(2).)
Neither of these conditions is true about either ofyour economic interests; therefore, they
are not directly involved in the decision about The Knolls lawsuit. Under the Commission’s
regulations, sources of income which are not directly involved under the rules stated above are
considered indirectly involved for purposes of choosing a materiality standard (see below).
(Regulation 18704.1(b).)
5. Dediding which materiality standards to use to decide if there will be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests.
Knowing that both of your relevant economic interests are indirectly involved in the
decision about The Knolls lawsuit, the next step is picking the appropriate standard for
evaluating the “materiality”—this is, the importance—of the effect of the decision on those
economic interests. (See Regulation 1 8700(b)(5).)
(a) The buyer ofyourformerpersonal residence.
We assume that this buyer is an individual (or individuals). When the source of income
to a public official is an individual, and that source is indirectly involved in the governmental
decision in question—as is the case here—the materiality standard in Regulation 1 8705.3(b)(3)
applies. It provides that the financial effect of a decision is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that either of the following will be true as a result ofthe decision:


File No. A-99-239
Page 7
•
The decision will affect the individual’s income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities—excluding real property interests—by $1,000 or more; or
•
The decision will affect real property owned by that individual in a manner which is
material under the tests in Regulation 18705.2(b).
Regulation 18705.2(b), to which the second prong refers, applies when real property
holdings are indirectly involved in a decision. Regulation 18705.2(b) has several alternative
rules; which one applies in a given case usually depends on the proximity of the real projjerty
interest to the property which is the subject of the governmental decision. Under the complex
and unusual facts of this case,
6 we advise that Regulation 18705 .2(b)(3), which covers situations
in which the exact proximity of the property which is the subject of the decision to the real
property owned by your source of income cannot be detennined, should apply. Subdivision
(b)(3) (by reference to subdivision (b)(1)(C)) provides that the financial effect of a decision is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that either of the following will be true as a result of the
decision:
•
The fair market value of the real property will increase or decrease by ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more; or
•
The rental value of the property will increase or decrease by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.
(b) The Rupert Taylor brokerage.
Based on the facts you have presented about the brokerage, the materiality standards in
Regulation 18705.1(b)(7) appear to apply. That subdivision provides that the financial effects of
a governmental decision are material if it is reasonably foreseeable that any ofthe following are
true as a result of the decision:
6 These facts are complex and unusual because your source of income, who is indirectly involved in
decisions about The Knolls lawsuit, is a member of the homeowners’ association which owns the common property
on which the upper wall would be built. That individual’s real property interests include a proportional share of any
common property owned by the homeowners’ association of which he or she is a member. (See LeRoy Advice
Letter, No. 1-97-592; Field Advice Letter, No. A-94-106.) Here, therefore, the individual who purchased your
home has an economic interest in the common properties owned by The Knolls homeowners’ association (assuming
his or her interest is worth $1,000 or more). Part of this common property is at-issue in The Knolls lawsuit (i.e., as
the site on which the upper wall would be built). From this, it could be argued that the individual who purchased
your home is actually directly, rather than indirectly, involved in the decision because real property in which he has
an interest is the subject of the lawsuit. However, under these facts, because it isthe property of your source of
income, not your property, that is the subject of the lawsuit, we believe it is appropriate to apply the materiality
standard for cases where the distance of the real property which is the economic interest to the property which is the
subject of the decision cannot be determined.


File No. A-99-239
Page 8
“(A) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross
revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
“(B) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year
in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
“(C) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of
assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.” (Regulation l8705.1(b)(7)(A)-(C).)
6. Using the materiality standards to decide if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on your economic interests.
The sixth, and usually most important step, in deciding whether you have a conffict of
interest is using the materiality standards (from step 5, above) to decide if a material financial
effect on one of more of your economic interests is reasonably foreseeable as a result of the
decision. (Regulation 18706.)
As used here, “reasonably foreseeable” means “substantially likely.” (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a governmental
decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made is highly situation-specific;
making this evaluation is a “judgment call.” A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the
standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Thid.)
(a) The buyer ofyourformerpersonal residence.
As to your economic interest in the buyer of your former personal residence, the
important questions are whether it is substantially likely that any of the following will be true as
a result of The Knolls lawsuit decision:
•
His or her income, investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities—excluding real property interests—will increase or decrease by $1,000 or
more; or
•
The fair market value of his or her real property will increase or decrease by ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more; or
•
The rental value of his or her real property will increase or decrease by $1,000 or more
per 12 month period.
For example, if it is substantially likely that each member of The Knolls homeowners’
association, including the individual who is a source of income to you, faces proportional
liability for legal fees arising from the lawsuit in the amount of $1,000 or more, then a conflict of
interest exists, unless the public generally exception applies. (The public generally exception is
discussed below.)


File No. A-99-239
Page 9
We are unable to answer these essentially factual questions conclusively from a distance.
Based on your knowledge of the facts about the lawsuit and the neighborhood in question, you
must make this determination. If you conclude that the answer to any of these three questions is
“yes,” then you have a conflict of interest unless the public generally exception applies. If the
answer to all three questions is “no,” then you do not have a conflict of interest arising from your
economic interest in the individual who purchased your former personal residence.
(b) The Rupert Taylor brokerage.
As to your economic interest in the Rupert Taylor brokerage, the important questions are
whether it is substantially likely that any of the following will be true as a result of The Knolls
lawsuit decision:
•
The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the brokerage’s gross revenues for a
fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
•
The decision will result in the brokerage incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or
more; or
•
The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value ofthe brokerage’s assets or
liabilities of $10,000 or more.
(Regulation 1 8705.1(b)(7)(A)-(C).)
You have told us that Rupert Taylor has no particular connection with The Knolls. It is
not, for example, an exclusive listing agent for the development, nor is an unusual amount of its
revenues derived from listings in The Knolls. You have presented no facts indicating that it is
substantially likely that governmental decisions about The Knolls lawsuit will have a financial
impact on Rupert Taylor which meets the materiality standards explained above. Further, you
have told us that you believe it unlikely that The Knolls lawsuit will have any appreciable impact
on Rupert. Therefore, we advise that you do not have a conflict of interest arising from your
economic interest in the Rupert Taylor brokerage.
7. The public generally exception.
Even if you conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions about The Knolls
lawsuit will have a material financial effect on the individual who purchased your former
personal residence (see part IV.B.6(a), above),
7 you do not have a conflict of interest if the public
generally exception applies. If the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on that
‘ We include this analysis to assist you if you decide that it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions about
The Knolls lawsuit will have a material fmancial effect on the individual who purchased your former residence. By
including this material we are not inferring that you necessarily should so decide; as we explained above, the answer
to that question depends on facts which we cannot grasp adequately from a distance.


File No. A-99-239
Page 10
individual is indistinguishable ‘from its effect on the public generally, “then you do not have a
conflict. (Section 87103; Regulations 1 8700(b)(7), 18707(a).)
This rule is referred to as the “public generally exception.” This exception exists because
a public official is less likely to be biased by a fmancial impact on his or her economic interests
when a significant part of the community is substantially likely to feel essentially the same
impact from the governmental decision.
Generally, the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official’s
economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it is also
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect a “significant segment” of the public “in
substantially the same manner” it will affect the public official’s economic interest. (Regulation
1 8707(b)(1),(2).) Applying the public generally exception requires two closely interrelated
judgments. Using rules found in the FPPC’s regulations, one must determine whether there is a
“significant segment” of the public which is likely to be affected by the governmental decision in
“substantially the same manner” as is the economic interest which is potentially creating the
conflict. (There are also “special purpose” versions of the public generally exception which may
apply in special factual situations. (See Regulations 18707.1
- 18707.6.))
In this case, a “significant segment” may be comprised of:
•
10 percent or more ofthe population in the jurisdiction of your agency or in your district.
(Regulation 18707(b)(1)(A)(i).)
•
10 percent or more of all property owners, all home owners or all households in the
jurisdiction of your agency or in your district. (Regulation 18707(b)(1)(A)(ii).)
•
At least 5,000 individuals who are residents ofthe jurisdiction. (Regulation
1 8707(b)(1)(C).)
In terms of your situation, even if council decisions about The Knolls lawsuit will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the individual who purchased your former
personal residence, you will not have a conflict fit is also reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a substantially similar financial effect on the following segments of your
jurisdiction or district: (1) 10 percent or more of the population, or (2) 10 percent or more of all
property owners, homeowners or households, or (3) at least 5,000 individuals.
C.
Issues surrounding Bayview.
You have asked whether the fact that you previously made a governmental decision about
several homes in the Bayview development now precludes you from taking listings in that
development. The answer is no. Past business activities may have conflict-of-interest
implications for upcoming governmental decisions because those business activies tend to create
the kinds of economic interests (e.g., sources of income to a public official) from which conflicts


File No. A-99-239
Page 11
can arise. However, by themselves, past governmental decisions do not generally preclude
future business activities.
8 Thus, simply having made a governmental decision about Bayview
does not, in and of itself, make Bayview “off-limits” to you as a real estate agent. Of course, we
cannot comment on whether your past conduct, in and of itself, resulted in a conflict of interest
violation. We can only comment as to your prospective activity.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven 0. Churchwell
O
eral Counsel
By
John Vegelli
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:IV:tls
8 A provision of the Act, called the “revolving door law,” forbids public officials, for a one-year period,
from attempting to influence their old agencies for paying clients. This law also permanently forbids former public
officials from “switching sides” to work for a paying client on a matter on which he or she worked in public service.
(See Section 87400 et seq.)
However, as expressed in the Act, these rules apply ‘only to state, not local officials.
Thus, they do not apply to you as a city councilmember. In any event, even if you were a state official, they would
not apply based on the facts you have provided.
I:\Advice Ltrs\99239.wpd

sections.question:
1. Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions regarding a lawsuit by the
City of Belmont against a private homeowners’ association, “The Knolls of Belmont”?
2. Does the Act prohibit you from taking real estate listings for properties in a certain
development, “Bayview at Belmont,” merely because you voted in the July 1999 city council
decision involving two particular properties in that development?
sections.conclusion:
1. The answer to this question depends primarily on whether council decisions about The
Knolls lawsuit will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the individual who
purchased your former residence in The Knolls, and who is thus a source of income to you.
Although we cannot make this essentially factual determination from a distance, we have
explained the specific factual questions you must answer to make this determination.Page 2
2. No. Merely having made a governmental decision about Bayview in the past does not,
in and of itself, preclude you from doing business in Bayview in the future.

B. Facts related to question no. 2.
In July 1999, you voted, as a city council member, on a matter involving the construction

sections.analysis:
A.
Introduction.
The Act’s conflict-ofinterest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own fmancial interests or the
financial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or othrwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
To say that a public official has a “financial interest” in a, governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, is to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official’s economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard,
eight-step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualif’ing conflict of interest in a
given governmental decision.
2 (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) The following advice applies that
standard analysis.
B.
The lawsuit involving The Knolls.
1.
You are covered by the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to “public officials.” (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) As a city coundil member, you are a “public official,”
for purposes of the Act (see Sections 82048, 82041), and the conflict-of-interest rules apply to
you.
2. Decisions about the lawsuit against The Knolls are covered by the Act’s conflict-of-
interest provisions.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official “make[sj,
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.”
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) The Commission has adopted a series of regulations
which defme “making,” “participating in making,” and “influencing” a governmental decision,
and which provide certain exceptions. (Regulations 18702-18702.4.)
2 The eighth step, which pertains to the “legally required participation” rule (see Regulation 18708),
applies only in rare cases where several public officials in the same agency are simultaneously disqualified. It is not
relevant to this advice request, and is not mentioned further.
By deliberating and voting in council decisions about the lawsuit against The Knolls, you
would be making (Regulation 18702.1), and participating in making (Regulation 18702.2), a
governmental decision. Thus, the Act’s conflict-of-interest rules apply to these decisions.
3. Identifying your relevant economic interests.
(a) Introduction
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The “economic interests” from which conflicts of interest may arise are defined in
Regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifying which, if any, ofthese economic interests are held by a
public official is the third step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest under the Act. (See
Regulation 18700(b)(3).) There are five kinds of such economic interests:
•
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment
3 of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position ofmanagement (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));
•
A public official has an economic interest in realproperty in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofincome, including promised
income, which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofgfts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4);
•
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those ofhis or her immediate family—this is known as the
“personal financial effects” rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).
An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the
official, the official’s agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)
(b) You have an economic interest in the person who bought your house in The Knolls.
When you sold your residence in The Knolls, the buyer became a source of income to
you. (See Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) Thus, you have an economic interest in that
buyer for twelve months following the transaction.
4
There are no facts, however, which indicate that you have an economic interest in The
Knolls itself (i.e., as a legal entity). Absent facts that indicate, for example, that the buyer of
your residence controls the homeowners’ association or faces unusual or unique liability in the
lawsuit, or that The Knolls has been a client of yours, there is no basis to conclude that you have
an economic interest in The Knolls as a legal entity.
(c) You have an economic interest in Rupert Taylor, the brokerage.
As an independent contractor, you have received income from the broker with whom you
are assoóiated, Rupert Taylor. Presumably, that income is at least $250 in the past twelve
months. Therefore, you have an economic interest in Rupert Taylor because it is a source of
income to you. (See Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
(d) Thefact that you may have listings in The Knolls in thefuture does not create any
economic interestsfor you right now.
You have asked about whether the fact that you may have real estate listings in The
Knolls in the future is relevant to whether you have a conflict of interst in the present decision
about the lawsuit against The Knolls. A public officiallreal estate agent’s listings can be relevant
because listings lead to income from the buying and selling of the listed property. “Income”
under the Act includes promised income, that is, income to which a public official has a legally
enforceable right. The person against whom the public official may enforce this legal right is the
source of the income.
We have consistently advised that merely having a listing on a property does not
constitute promised income. However, once a sale is pending on the listed property, the client is
deemed to be a source of promised income.
5 Merely having a listing on a property does not
constitute promised income because a listing is too speculative to rise to the level ofpromised
income. However, once escrow is opened, there is a buyer and a seller, and the element of
speculation has diminished.
Although a public official need not disclose his or her personal residence on his or her Statement of
Economic Interests (FPPC Form 700) (See Section 87206(f)), a personal residence may nonetheless cause a conflict
of interest. In other words, although you may disregard your personal residence for disclosure purposes, you must
consider it for purposes of deciding whether you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given decision.
See the Bergman Advice Letter, No. A-93-297, Clay Advice Letter, No. 1-96-33 5, Leichter Advice
Letter, No. 1-98-173, and the Weiler Advice Letter, No. A-99-060.
4. Determining the degree to which your economic interests are involved in the lawsuit
against The Knolls.
The fourth step in analyzing whether you have a conflict of interest is to determine
whether your respective economic interests are directly or indirectly involved in the decisions
about The Knolls lawsuit. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) This step is important because it helps
determine (in the flflh step) which test for materiality to use in deciding whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the economic interets.
In this case, your two relevant economic interests, the buyer of your former personal
residence and Rupert Taylor are sources of income to you. A source of income is directly
involved in a decision when that person:
•
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704.1(a)(1)); or
•
Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation 18704.1 (a)(2).)
Neither of these conditions is true about either ofyour economic interests; therefore, they
are not directly involved in the decision about The Knolls lawsuit. Under the Commission’s
regulations, sources of income which are not directly involved under the rules stated above are
considered indirectly involved for purposes of choosing a materiality standard (see below).
(Regulation 18704.1(b).)
5. Dediding which materiality standards to use to decide if there will be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests.
Knowing that both of your relevant economic interests are indirectly involved in the
decision about The Knolls lawsuit, the next step is picking the appropriate standard for
evaluating the “materiality”—this is, the importance—of the effect of the decision on those
economic interests. (See Regulation 1 8700(b)(5).)
(a) The buyer ofyourformerpersonal residence.
We assume that this buyer is an individual (or individuals). When the source of income
to a public official is an individual, and that source is indirectly involved in the governmental
decision in question—as is the case here—the materiality standard in Regulation 1 8705.3(b)(3)
applies. It provides that the financial effect of a decision is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that either of the following will be true as a result ofthe decision:
•
The decision will affect the individual’s income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities—excluding real property interests—by $1,000 or more; or
•
The decision will affect real property owned by that individual in a manner which is
material under the tests in Regulation 18705.2(b).
Regulation 18705.2(b), to which the second prong refers, applies when real property
holdings are indirectly involved in a decision. Regulation 18705.2(b) has several alternative
rules; which one applies in a given case usually depends on the proximity of the real projjerty
interest to the property which is the subject of the governmental decision. Under the complex
and unusual facts of this case,
6 we advise that Regulation 18705 .2(b)(3), which covers situations
in which the exact proximity of the property which is the subject of the decision to the real
property owned by your source of income cannot be detennined, should apply. Subdivision
(b)(3) (by reference to subdivision (b)(1)(C)) provides that the financial effect of a decision is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that either of the following will be true as a result of the
decision:
•
The fair market value of the real property will increase or decrease by ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more; or
•
The rental value of the property will increase or decrease by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.
(b) The Rupert Taylor brokerage.
Based on the facts you have presented about the brokerage, the materiality standards in
Regulation 18705.1(b)(7) appear to apply. That subdivision provides that the financial effects of
a governmental decision are material if it is reasonably foreseeable that any ofthe following are
true as a result of the decision:
6 These facts are complex and unusual because your source of income, who is indirectly involved in
decisions about The Knolls lawsuit, is a member of the homeowners’ association which owns the common property
on which the upper wall would be built. That individual’s real property interests include a proportional share of any
common property owned by the homeowners’ association of which he or she is a member. (See LeRoy Advice
Letter, No. 1-97-592; Field Advice Letter, No. A-94-106.) Here, therefore, the individual who purchased your
home has an economic interest in the common properties owned by The Knolls homeowners’ association (assuming
his or her interest is worth $1,000 or more). Part of this common property is at-issue in The Knolls lawsuit (i.e., as
the site on which the upper wall would be built). From this, it could be argued that the individual who purchased
your home is actually directly, rather than indirectly, involved in the decision because real property in which he has
an interest is the subject of the lawsuit. However, under these facts, because it isthe property of your source of
income, not your property, that is the subject of the lawsuit, we believe it is appropriate to apply the materiality
standard for cases where the distance of the real property which is the economic interest to the property which is the
subject of the decision cannot be determined.
“(A) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross
revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
“(B) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year
in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
“(C) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of
assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.” (Regulation l8705.1(b)(7)(A)-(C).)
6. Using the materiality standards to decide if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on your economic interests.
The sixth, and usually most important step, in deciding whether you have a conffict of
interest is using the materiality standards (from step 5, above) to decide if a material financial
effect on one of more of your economic interests is reasonably foreseeable as a result of the
decision. (Regulation 18706.)
As used here, “reasonably foreseeable” means “substantially likely.” (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a governmental
decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made is highly situation-specific;
making this evaluation is a “judgment call.” A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the
standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Thid.)
(a) The buyer ofyourformerpersonal residence.
As to your economic interest in the buyer of your former personal residence, the
important questions are whether it is substantially likely that any of the following will be true as
a result of The Knolls lawsuit decision:
•
His or her income, investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities—excluding real property interests—will increase or decrease by $1,000 or
more; or
•
The fair market value of his or her real property will increase or decrease by ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more; or
•
The rental value of his or her real property will increase or decrease by $1,000 or more
per 12 month period.
For example, if it is substantially likely that each member of The Knolls homeowners’
association, including the individual who is a source of income to you, faces proportional
liability for legal fees arising from the lawsuit in the amount of $1,000 or more, then a conflict of
interest exists, unless the public generally exception applies. (The public generally exception is
discussed below.)
We are unable to answer these essentially factual questions conclusively from a distance.
Based on your knowledge of the facts about the lawsuit and the neighborhood in question, you
must make this determination. If you conclude that the answer to any of these three questions is
“yes,” then you have a conflict of interest unless the public generally exception applies. If the
answer to all three questions is “no,” then you do not have a conflict of interest arising from your
economic interest in the individual who purchased your former personal residence.
(b) The Rupert Taylor brokerage.
As to your economic interest in the Rupert Taylor brokerage, the important questions are
whether it is substantially likely that any of the following will be true as a result of The Knolls
lawsuit decision:
•
The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the brokerage’s gross revenues for a
fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
•
The decision will result in the brokerage incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or
more; or
•
The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value ofthe brokerage’s assets or
liabilities of $10,000 or more.
(Regulation 1 8705.1(b)(7)(A)-(C).)
You have told us that Rupert Taylor has no particular connection with The Knolls. It is
not, for example, an exclusive listing agent for the development, nor is an unusual amount of its
revenues derived from listings in The Knolls. You have presented no facts indicating that it is
substantially likely that governmental decisions about The Knolls lawsuit will have a financial
impact on Rupert Taylor which meets the materiality standards explained above. Further, you
have told us that you believe it unlikely that The Knolls lawsuit will have any appreciable impact
on Rupert. Therefore, we advise that you do not have a conflict of interest arising from your
economic interest in the Rupert Taylor brokerage.
7. The public generally exception.
Even if you conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions about The Knolls
lawsuit will have a material financial effect on the individual who purchased your former
personal residence (see part IV.B.6(a), above),
7 you do not have a conflict of interest if the public
generally exception applies. If the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on that
‘ We include this analysis to assist you if you decide that it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions about
The Knolls lawsuit will have a material fmancial effect on the individual who purchased your former residence. By
including this material we are not inferring that you necessarily should so decide; as we explained above, the answer
to that question depends on facts which we cannot grasp adequately from a distance.
individual is indistinguishable ‘from its effect on the public generally, “then you do not have a
conflict. (Section 87103; Regulations 1 8700(b)(7), 18707(a).)
This rule is referred to as the “public generally exception.” This exception exists because
a public official is less likely to be biased by a fmancial impact on his or her economic interests
when a significant part of the community is substantially likely to feel essentially the same
impact from the governmental decision.
Generally, the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official’s
economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it is also
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect a “significant segment” of the public “in
substantially the same manner” it will affect the public official’s economic interest. (Regulation
1 8707(b)(1),(2).) Applying the public generally exception requires two closely interrelated
judgments. Using rules found in the FPPC’s regulations, one must determine whether there is a
“significant segment” of the public which is likely to be affected by the governmental decision in
“substantially the same manner” as is the economic interest which is potentially creating the
conflict. (There are also “special purpose” versions of the public generally exception which may
apply in special factual situations. (See Regulations 18707.1
- 18707.6.))
In this case, a “significant segment” may be comprised of:
•
10 percent or more ofthe population in the jurisdiction of your agency or in your district.
(Regulation 18707(b)(1)(A)(i).)
•
10 percent or more of all property owners, all home owners or all households in the
jurisdiction of your agency or in your district. (Regulation 18707(b)(1)(A)(ii).)
•
At least 5,000 individuals who are residents ofthe jurisdiction. (Regulation
1 8707(b)(1)(C).)
In terms of your situation, even if council decisions about The Knolls lawsuit will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the individual who purchased your former
personal residence, you will not have a conflict fit is also reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a substantially similar financial effect on the following segments of your
jurisdiction or district: (1) 10 percent or more of the population, or (2) 10 percent or more of all
property owners, homeowners or households, or (3) at least 5,000 individuals.
C.
Issues surrounding Bayview.
You have asked whether the fact that you previously made a governmental decision about
several homes in the Bayview development now precludes you from taking listings in that
development. The answer is no. Past business activities may have conflict-of-interest
implications for upcoming governmental decisions because those business activies tend to create
the kinds of economic interests (e.g., sources of income to a public official) from which conflicts
can arise. However, by themselves, past governmental decisions do not generally preclude
future business activities.
8 Thus, simply having made a governmental decision about Bayview
does not, in and of itself, make Bayview “off-limits” to you as a real estate agent. Of course, we
cannot comment on whether your past conduct, in and of itself, resulted in a conflict of interest
violation. We can only comment as to your prospective activity.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions regarding a lawsuit by the
City of Belmont against a private homeowners’ association, “The Knolls of Belmont”?
2. Does the Act prohibit you from taking real estate listings for properties in a certain
development, “Bayview at Belmont,” merely because you voted in the July 1999 city council
decision involving two particular properties in that development?

CONCLUSION: 1. The answer to this question depends primarily on whether council decisions about The
Knolls lawsuit will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the individual who
purchased your former residence in The Knolls, and who is thus a source of income to you.
Although we cannot make this essentially factual determination from a distance, we have
explained the specific factual questions you must answer to make this determination.Page 2
2. No. Merely having made a governmental decision about Bayview in the past does not,
in and of itself, preclude you from doing business in Bayview in the future.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street Sacramento. CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-5660 Fax (916) 322-0886 October 1, 1999 Eleanore Hahn City of Belmont Belmont City Council 1070 Sixth Avenue Belmont, California 94002-3893 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-99-239 Dear Ms. Hahn: This letter responds to your request for advice about the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 I. QUESTIONS 1. Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions regarding a lawsuit by the City of Belmont against a private homeowners’ association, “The Knolls of Belmont”? 2. Does the Act prohibit you from taking real estate listings for properties in a certain development, “Bayview at Belmont,” merely because you voted in the July 1999 city council decision involving two particular properties in that development? II. CONCLUSIONS 1. The answer to this question depends primarily on whether council decisions about The Knolls lawsuit will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the individual who purchased your former residence in The Knolls, and who is thus a source of income to you. Although we cannot make this essentially factual determination from a distance, we have explained the specific factual questions you must answer to make this determination. Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-99-239 Page 2 2. No. Merely having made a governmental decision about Bayview in the past does not, in and of itself, preclude you from doing business in Bayview in the future. III. FACTS You are a city councihnember for the City of Belmont (the “city”). In your private life, you work in real estate sales. You are associated with a real estate brokerage, Rupert Ta’lor, as an independent contractor. A. Facts related to question no. 1. In early 1998, there were several severe slides in the hills of Belmont. One of the slides occurred in the Hallmark area of Belmont when a portion of the hillside belonging to a private homeowners’ association, “The Knolls ofBelmont” (The Knolls), fell into and clogged a drainage ditch (“V” Ditch). The clogged drainage ditch caused water to cascade onto the hill below, weakening the hill and causing a portion of the hillside to cave into a home, thus destroying the rear master bedroom and bath area ofthat home. The city awarded a contract for repair of this bill and below the “V” Ditch to prevent a future occurrence. The lower wall has been built; however, the upper wall cannot be constructed without permission from The Knolls, since the upper wall would be on their property. The Board of The Knolls instructed their attorney to not communicate in any way with Belmont’s city attorney. A lawsuit was filed by the City ofBelmont against The Knolls, claiming negligence. You anticipate that you will take real estate listings in The Knolls in the near future; however, at the present time, you have no listings there. Rupert Taylor has
